misconceptionSummary: Students think multiplying always makes a number bigger and dividing always makes it smaller.

visuals.ruleText: Multiplying by a number less than 1 makes the result smaller

steps.narration: Multiplication does not always increase a value. Multiplying by a fraction between zero and one shrinks the result. Twenty times one half is ten — smaller than twenty.

lines.text: Dividing by a number < 1 increases the result

steps.narration: Dividing by a number less than one makes the result bigger. Twenty divided by one half asks how many halves fit in twenty — the answer is forty. Division can increase a value.

lines.text: 1 is the identity for multiplication and division

steps.narration: One is the identity for multiplication and division. Multiplying or dividing any number by one leaves it unchanged. This is the boundary: multiply by more than one to grow, less than one to shrink.

visuals.label: Effect depends on the multiplier/divisor, not just the operation

steps.narration: The effect of multiplying or dividing depends entirely on the number you use — not just the operation symbol. Always check whether the multiplier is greater or less than one.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: assuming multiplication always increases a number. 20 × 0.5 = 10 — multiplying by less than 1 shrinks. 20 ÷ 0.5 = 40 — dividing by less than 1 grows.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Multiplication can shrink and division can grow. It depends on whether the second number is greater or less than 1 — not on which symbol you use.
